dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, chest pain and vomiting, which started this morning.
I was woken up by the chest pain, and I've had intermittent nausea and vomiting all day with only some belly tenderness.
Can you describe the pain and how it feels right now?
Yes, the chest pain is sharp, right in the center, and radiates to my left side. I'd rate it a 10 out of 10.
I've had intermittent nausea and vomiting all day with no active vomiting now, but my stomach is currently tender from the vomiting.
You mentioned you already took some medication. What did you take?
I took 324 mg of aspirin already.
You're alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation-this is a GCS of 15.
You open your eyes spontaneously and can follow moving objects around you.
You speak clearly and understand what's being asked and your location.
You can follow directions with your body movements and respond physically to cues.
I'm checking your airway for any signs of blockage or unusual sounds.
Your breathing is regular, 18 breaths per minute, and not labored at all.
Your pulse is strong, and your skin looks normal with no unusual color changes.
Your heart rate is 84 beats per minute upon initial assessment.
Your respiratory rate is 18 and shows no signs of distress.
Ma'am, we're going to take your blood pressure now. Your first reading is 145 over 104.
We just ran a 4-lead ECG as part of our cardiac evaluation.
You're not showing signs of paleness or diaphoretic changes.
Your pupils appear equal in size and react normally to light changes.
Your oxygen saturation is 98%.
I notice you haven't mentioned any shortness of breath, cough, or fever. Have you been around anyone with those symptoms recently?
No, I haven't had any known exposure to anyone with those symptoms recently.
Can you tell me about any past health conditions or surgeries you've had?
I have a history of hypertension, stroke, a past heart attack, and I deal with depression and anxiety with regular medication.
Do you have any known allergies?
No, I don't have any known allergies from the list we've discussed.
What medications are you currently taking?
I've been taking insulin and other medicines for diabetes and mental health conditions.
Was there any food or drink before the symptoms started?
I don't remember the exact timing of my last meal or drink before the chest pain hit.
Can you tell me what was happening when you started feeling unwell?
I was asleep when I woke up with the chest pain. I had been vomiting off and on all morning before this.
The chest pain you felt was sudden during the night, correct?
Does the pain change with movement or deep breaths at all?
You say the pain is sharp. Does it have any other descriptions like burning or heavy?
Is the pain continuing to radiate elsewhere, or is it steady on the left side as now?
You rated it a 10 out of 10. Has it increased or decreased since it first started?
Have you noticed any morning or evening patterns to the pain or any worsening at specific times of day?
We completed a 12-lead EKG which shows a sinus rhythm with some abnormalities, potentially related to prior heart damage.
Based on your symptoms and these results, we recommend transportation to [COMPANY_NAME] for further evaluation.
You've been safely secured onto the stretcher and are ready for transport to the ambulance.
All findings and patient information have been communicated to the hospital team for your arrival.
Your vitals remain stable-pulse increased to 125, BP 125/82, and O2 saturation at 99% en route.
Has there been any change in your chest pain or development of new symptoms since we began monitoring you?
No, it's still sharp and 10/10, and there's no new discomfort I've noticed lately.
Your abdomen remains tender but there's no active vomiting to report.
Your breathing and pulse have remained steady during transit.
Your blood sugar is still elevated at 487, consistent with your history of insulin-dependent diabetes.
We're ongoing monitoring your condition for any changes as we approach the hospital.
Your vital findings, medical history, and EKG, have all been documented for the suspected cardiac condition protocol.
Based on your elevated BGL, EKG, and pain, we are following protocol 2-1 for suspected cardiac issues.
You've been safely transferred into the ambulance and secured for the journey with ongoing monitoring.
[COMPANY_NAME] has been contacted and has been updated on your elevated blood sugar, chest pain, and cardiac medical history.
We were unable to start an IV line, but we completed the ECGs and moved you to transport as per protocol.
You haven't received any additional medications, and you're maintaining a strong and regular heartbeat.
Your breathing and oxygen levels have remained stable throughout the transport with no changes observed.
You're doing well for now and will be smoothly handed over to the hospital emergency team upon arrival for further care.
